sentences.text: Courage, I mentioned before.
I'm repeating myself, guts, courage.
It was two ways to go, you or myself.
And this was the, the de-- how do you say-- the, the, the destination, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not to go to a gas chamber, so I fought my way out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sentenced to Siberia.
I fought my way out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in the underground.
I got out.
I was in the army.
I tried to make my survival.
And everything was courage.
Everything was day by day.
I used to go in the first-- in the army, go down in-- to get my food.
And I went with two, three soldiers, and bang, bang, bang.
And I walked.
I even didn't bend down.
We got used to it.
You know, it was yes, yes, no, no.
You want to live, there's no such a thing how poor you are and what conditions you are.
Your always hope was to get up the next day and survive.
And this--
OK, in the DP camps, we found out that I am on a French quota.
In the meantime, Molly, my wife's sister, and sister got papers from Canada to go to Canada.
And then one morning, we get up in the morning, ready to take my sister, which Ann didn't go with me.
I took her to a camp.
From there, they were transferred to Bremerhaven, to the German port to take them to England, to Liverpool.
From there, they immigrated into Halifax, I think, to Canada.
And I take my sister, and I said goodbye.
It was Bamberg.
Bamberg was the camp where I took her and said goodbye.
And I went home, back to my wife.
And on the way in a town Plattling, where the trains comes in from Nuremberg, train comes in from Munich, and then we, we go into another train, go into Deggendorf.
And I met a man coming in from Munich.
And he said, Hollander, I'm just coming from Munich, and your papers are ready for immigration to the United States.
So I came home.
I slept over the night, and the next day, I got up.
And I went back to Munich and picked up my visas, my papers, to go, which the HIAS already helped us, paying for us to come to the United States.
The HIAS paid.
The Hebrew-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] HIAS is the Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society.
They helped us, which I was very thankful to them.
And If you go to [INAUDIBLE], Philadelphia, you'll see my name in the HIAS, all over.
I did try to repay their efforts, what they did for us.
I have a lot of letters.
I showed it to you before.
And there we were sent to a resettling camp from Deggendorf to the [INAUDIBLE], they called it.
It was a German military camp.
From there, we also went to Bremerhaven, to the port of Germany.
And Ann became pregnant.
Now, I didn't want to have a child on the German soil.
As young as I was, my mind worked.
I didn't want to have on the German soil, children.
And as soon we found out that we are getting out from Germany, my papers coming through, she became pregnant.
A matter of fact, she was in the seventh, or the sixth month going on the boat.
So we knew that our child will be born in United States.
11 days, and Ann was very sick.
No, we were separated.
This was a military, an army boat, Marine Tiger, 12,000 ton.
A matter of fact, yesterday, when we went to the Philadelphia gathering, I met a friend of mine, which she came with us on the same boat so many years ago.
But we are friendly.
And we still reminding ourselves about Marine Tiger, that my Anni was so sick on that boat, being pregnant yet.
We thought that she's going to lose the baby.
And I managed how to help her out, to bring her an orange.
We couldn't go to the kitchen.
The boat was-- the last few days.
December is the worst weather, you know.
Came close to the shores of the United States, that boat was shaking, 20-feet-high waves.
And there was a lot of experience for us, you know, the first time on a boat.
We came into the United States Christmas Eve.
They couldn't bring us into the port because they want to give the crew off for the Christmas Eve.
So we were on the water, not far from the Statue of Liberty.
And we went out on the deck.
The waters were quiet, nice.
And we see so many cars going, so many cars from a distance.
I thought that cars coming out from the ocean going into New York.
You know, those years, we didn't see those cars in Europe.
In all those years, we never knew about cars, you  know.
If anybody who had a car in Europe or a truck, was rich people.
But the next day, in the morning, Christmas morning, I had relatives, cousins of my mother and aunt, but nobody waited for us.
So they took us off the boat.
And they brought us into the HIAS, headquarters of the HIAS.
It was 425 Lafayette Street, in Manhattan, in lower Manhattan.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I being so hungry, not eating on the boat for three days, for being sick, I had 15 hard-boiled eggs.
And they located us.
It took a few days.
They gave us a little home together with me and Anni to begin with for three days, four days until they get everything together.
Ann slept with woman, and we slept with men, you  know.
Then they got for us a room.
As people start to leave, people came, and people left, located people.
And the United Jewish Service, we went for appointments, and they were trying to locate us.
And those years, they didn't want to let together all the Jewish people in New York.
New York is a big Jewish population.
So they want the Jewish people to spread us over the United States.
I was supposed to go to Milwaukee.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, then I said, I have relatives in New York.
I want to come and see them.
And I would appreciate if some was closer to the choice was Philadelphia.
Very good.
We had our room.
And the Jewish Family Service gave, gave us an allowance weekly.
And they paid $12 to those people for the room.
The man was a Jewish policeman.
And to me, was a novelty and was something new that Jewish people were policemen in the United States.
Because being in the HIAS, we had a little congregation there, a little-- a little bit synagogue.
And I remember Jewish policeman came in there, and they had yahrzeit, say kaddish.
They came in, and, yeah, I see Jewish policeman.
In our lives in Poland, to see a Jewish policeman, we could never dream of it.
Finally, we took the baby out.
And one of the aunts from Canada visited us.
Her mother's sister came down.
And then we found two rooms.
Nobody wanted to take us in with a baby.
Were a hard time, those years.
Wasn't that helpful, the American Jewish community that way.
But we found two little homes.
I found a job.
Tailor.
I went back to the community.
I told them, thank you.
I-- I'm capable to support myself.
Didn't take long.
I moved from the two rooms to a house, rented.
I opened a cleaning store, didn't take long.
When I start to progress, little by little-- and I worked the shops and took care of the cleaning store.
We lived with it.
Those years we lived in the same houses where we had businesses.
Today is a different story.
And gradually, gradually, one child, and we had the other child.
And I progressed.
I bought myself a shop, a ladies shop, somebody to manufacture.
Sorry, that I couldn't make the success.
I didn't have enough capital to succeed.
And I used to be a good cutter.
The same customers who gave me to clean their suits and their dresses, I made them drapes and slipcovers later.
And then I moved a little bit better.
And then I opened a store, which I was over 20 years in one store.
Had a big fabric store, and this was the business.
I experienced-- during the war, I couldn't have enough experience to see the difference, because in Russian, religion was not permanent.
So what I did, after the war in Deggendorf, I went back to my old beliefs.
I got tefillin and start to go to the shul, normally.
And the tallis.
I was married with Orthodox way.
She was walking around seven times.
And was done the proper ritual way, the way I was dreamt to be, to have it done.
We came in here to Philadelphia, had the three children and raised them very well.
They had their children.
They got married fine and nice.
I'm happy I decided to make a move to Florida to retire.
Children weren't too happy, but we see each other.
We're close together.
And I have to make my way to make.
If I have to live, whatever God will give me, I want to live comfortable.
Since they were four, five years of age.
Each time we sat down to the table, before we eat, and during we ate, and after we ate, that story, the Holocaust, was right on top of the table.
This was a, a cocktail and a dessert.
We never missed a day.
Each piece of bread I put in my mouth, I told them that sometimes, this was not bread.
They knew.
They knew.
Everyone knew.
My son is very active.
He's very active in the second generation.
My children, they give their time to it.
I was very active in the community, in the Federation, Jewish Family Service.
I was active in the Speaker's Bureau.
I was in the [INAUDIBLE].
I was president of a shul and twice president of the Association of Jewish Holocaust survivors.
Till now, whatever I can do for the cause, I will continue as long as I live.
You're welcome.
Yeah, go ahead.
You want me to show you a paper?
If you want to look at it-- from Deggendorf?
If not-- it's 40, 50 years old.
Yes, this is, on my right side, my wife, Anni, which I have her for 49 years to 120.
She gave me a good life.
She gave me all my pride I needed and all my support for the years, and hope to the years to come.
This is a picture taken in public school in fourth grade.
The teacher name is Goldstein.
And many of our friends are alive from this picture.
And I am in the bottom line, sitting, the second from left, the cutest little boy in this picture.
Issy Hollander, picture was taken 1931 in Bedzin, in a public school in fourth grade.
This picture was taken 1930 in Bedzin, in public school.
And the teacher was a woman, [INAUDIBLE] [?
Luvna. ?] She is in the back, right next to the wall.
And some of my friends from this picture are still alive.
Sometimes we see each other.
It's been so many years since then.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This is my sister, my older sister, four years older than I am.
She perished in the Holocaust with four children.
And this is a picture that we found after the war from the archives to Canada.
I would've never had this picture if I wouldn't have be able to search for that.
She was born 1916, not far from Bedzin in Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The place was-- her name was Fay, and it was [?
Tzipporah, ?] Holender, H-O-L-E-N-D-E-R, Bedzin, Poland.
And the date of the photo, I cannot recall because it was taken during the German occupation, which I wasn't home.
This picture-- my name is Isadore Hollander in this picture-- was taken during the war in an unknown place.
I was in German in the first line.
And I cannot recall the date.
It was during the line-- during the time in the war.
It's the Polish army.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again, in here, I'm Isadore Hollander, sitting with a group of soldiers, Polish army, next to the left, sitting behind me, is my sergeant, which was a-- one of, I mentioned before, the biggest antisemite, which I was always afraid of him.
And this was taken the picture a day after the, the, the war ended.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] The war ended May the 8th, 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Again, this is me, Isadore Hollander being a policemen, a security guard in a DP camp in Deggendorf, in Germany after the war.
We didn't have any live ammunition or guns.
We just, for security purposes, to guard the camp.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Why, again, I am Isadore Hollander explaining the first newspaper after liberation in a DP camp in Deggendorf, and the dating over there for the first Hanukkah celebration in freedom since the war.
And here, this paper has been 50 years old.
[PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] Isadore Hollander again, and I am speaking in Philadelphia in front of an audience on the cemetery.
We are celebrating a year outside, which we have over there a shrine, a monument, for our fallen relatives.
All their names are inscribed over there, and I'm speaking on that particular day.
Every year, we are celebrating our anniversary for our loss of our families.
Again, my name is Isadore Hollander, as I mentioned before, we had in Philadelphia yearly symposiums with all kind of schools, Catholic, parochial, and Jewish schools.
And here, I was a speaker in the Speaker's Bureau.
And I'm sitting and speaking to the class about the Holocaust.
And the teacher is sitting beside me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My name, again, is Isadore Hollander.
Standing next to me is the ex-governor of Pennsylvania, Dick Thornburgh.
And I'm presenting to him a plaque, 40 years' liberation from our concentration camps.
This is a picture taken, Isadore Hollander with Anni Hollander, together in Philadelphia and the Association of Jewish Holocaust Survivors.
It was a souvenir plaque to a tribute dinner for Israel [INAUDIBLE], 30 years, the existence of the state of Israel.
Those people are with me is my chairman of the evening, Henry [?
Sloane. ?] Next to-- between my wife is the co-chair person, Rose [?
Bernstein. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture taken for me, and-- Issy Hollander and Anni Hollander, being given a plaque from the Jewish National Front.
It was a dinner in our names given.
On the left side is the chairman of the Jewish-- the chairperson of the Jewish National Front, Committee, Lily Tauss.
It's a cousin of mine.
Next to her is Harry Best, president of the association.
It's the picture.
Person is from the Jewish National Front, the lady next to Harry Best.
The chairman of the evening is a friend of mine, Sugarman, Ralph Sugarman, which he went to school with me together in Poland.
And the last one at right is the president of the ladies group in the association, Rose Bernstein.
This picture was taken.
It's a documentary picture.
And the document on top, it's myself, Isadore Hollander, taken in Germany 50 years ago.
And I am standing in front, holding the picture up.
We were celebrating the liberation and the cooperation from the HIAS, which they helped to take down the people, DP people from Germany.
We're standing in front of the Statue of Liberty in New Jersey, in the United States.
And I'm holding up the document, which was given to me in Germany as a member of the HIAS going to the United States.
This picture was taken in Harrisburg, Pennsylvania, as we commemorate every year the anniversary of the Shoah.
We are invited to the mention into the capital of the states.
And here, we are celebrating the anniversary, which, from left, is my daughter, Miriam, one of my grandsons, Stephen, myself, Isadore, my little granddaughter.
Behind is my wife, Anni, my daughter next to her, Shirley, and two little grandchildrens, Dina and Justin.
